coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots as a nanoprobe for analysis of o- and p-nitrophenols in environmental water adopting conventional fluorometry and smartphone image processing-assisted paper-based analytical device. In-depth study of sensing mechanisms
coredata.dc:creator: El-Shaheny, Rania
Yoshida, Sakura
Fuchigami, Takeshi
coredata.dc:description: A selective, sensitive, and safe nanoprobe was developed for detection of the endocrine disruptors; o- and p-nitrophenols (ONP and PNP, respectively) based on blue luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The two isomers efficiently quench the intense fluorescence of GQDs (λex/λem of 350/440 nm). A thorough study evidenced that the fluorescence quenching by ONP and PNP originates from inner filter effect and dynamic quenching. Additionally, the quenching sphere of action contributes to the quenching mechanisms at high concentrations of ONP. The fluorescence nanoprobe shows excellent linearity over wide dynamic ranges of 0.30–60.0 and 0.20–40.0 µg/mL with limits of quantification of 0.23 and 0.11 µg/mL and limits of detection of 0.07 and 0.03 µg/mL for ONP and PNP, respectively. The sensing system was fully validated and successfully used for detection of ONP and PNP in real samples from tap, river, and rainwaters showing excellent selectivity and recovery (98.5–102.0%). A smartphone image processing-assisted paper-based analytical device has been also designed employing a free software as an easily handled and portable device for fast in-field analysis of environmental waters. Moreover, ImageJ software coupled to a computer was applied for image processing to validate the newly adopted smartphone powered software. Statistical comparison of the data obtained by the two software programs showed no significant difference. This platform is very important and interesting specially for the developing countries lacking the facilities and infrastructure including advanced analytical laboratories and instruments. Such tactic allows these countries to keep pace with the increasing global requirements for environmental and human protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Nitrophenols
Environmental water
Paper-based probe
Image processing
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for analysis of real water samples
Fabrication of paper-based sensor coupled with image processing as a fast analysis tool for ONP and PNP
Applications of the designed probe for determination of ONP and PNP in real water samples
Fast analysis of ONP and PNP using paper-based analytical device assisted with image processing via smartphone- or computer-powered free software

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FTIR spectrum of GQDs, (B) Zeta potential curve of GQDs, and (C) A co-plot showing UV-absorption spectrum (A), excitation (EX), and emission (EM) spectra of GQDs, the inset illustrates a photo of GQDs under visible (left) and UV-radiation at 365 nm (right).

Fluorescence spectra of GQDs in presence of increasing concentrations of (A) ONP and (B) PNP, the insets are photos of GQDs in absence (left) and presence (right) of nitrophenol. (C) A co-plot showing the UV-absorption spectra of ONP and PNP and the excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of GQDs showing great overlap. Suppressed efficiency of observed and corrected fluorescence of GQDs after addition of different concentrations of (D) PNP and (E) ONP. The Stern-Volmer plots for (F) PNP and (G) ONP, the inset in (G) shows the linear fitting for concentrations up to 100 µM ONP. (H) The UV-absorption spectra of GQDs (using water as a reference) and GQDs-ONP (using ONP as a reference).
Effects of (A) pH and (B) GQDs concentration on fluorescence quenching efficiency in presence of ONP (14.4 µM) and PNP (14.4 µM).

Plots of fluorescence ratio F0/F versus concentrations of (A) PNP and (B) ONP, the insets show the linearity fitting over narrow concentration ranges of the corresponding curves. Plots of LnF0/F versus concentrations of (C) PNP and (D) ONP.

(A) Infographic illustration of the main steps to elucidate the mechanisms of fluorescence quenching of GQDs and (B) The mechanisms contributed to the quenching of GQDs fluorescence by nitrophenols (Q is the quencher and F is GQDs).
Illustration of the steps for image processing-assisted paper-based sensing of ONP and PNP in water samples (PBD means paper-based device).
Parameters of the Stern-Volmer relationship and the quenching sphere of action for the interaction of ONP and PNP with GQDs at different temperature settings.

row.entry: Volume of sphere of action, V (M−1)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of linearity, range, accuracy, and precision study for the two nitrophenols by the developed probe using the conventional fluorometry approach.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Three repeated determination/concentration in the same day.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Three repeated determinations on three consecutive days.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of different potential coexisting species on the determination of ONP and PNP by the proposed probe.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: %Quenching efficiency change = [(F0/F)2 - (F0/F)1] × 100 /(F0/F)1, where [(F0/F)1 and [(F0/F)2 are the quenching efficiency without and with the interfering substance, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the developed method with some reported relevant fluorometric methods.

row.entry: Synthesis of the membrane and a special flow-through measuring cell are needed
Lengthy and multi-step synthesis needs ≈ 58 h
Lengthy and multi-step synthesis needs ≈ 48 h
Conventional fluorometry and image processing-assisted paper-based device
Commercially available, portable, easily handled, and simple analytical device

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results for determination of ONP and PNP in different environmental waters by the proposed probe. a.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Data are expressed in µg/mL to facilitate direct comparison with the reported limit values [45].

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each result is the average of three individual determinations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Statistical comparison of the results for determination of nitrophenols in real water samples.
Validation parameters and statistical evaluation of the paper-based analytical device for determination of ONP and PNP using the two studied software.

list-item.label: (C)
list-item.para: Statistical comparison of the results obtained by smartphone-powered and computer-powered software

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each result is the mean value of three independent measurements.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values between the parentheses are the critical values of t and F [50].

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots as a nanoprobe for analysis of o- and p-nitrophenols in environmental water adopting conventional fluorometry and smartphone image processing-assisted paper-based analytical device. In-depth study of sensing mechanisms

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Hygienic Chemistry, Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences, Nagasaki University, Nagasaki, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Hygienic Chemistry, Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences, Nagasaki University, Nagasaki, Japan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt (R. El-Shaheny); Department of Hygienic Chemistry, Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences, Nagasaki University, Nagasaki, Japan (T. Fuchigami).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A selective, sensitive, and safe nanoprobe was developed for detection of the endocrine disruptors; o- and p-nitrophenols (ONP and PNP, respectively) based on blue luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The two isomers efficiently quench the intense fluorescence of GQDs (λex/λem of 350/440 nm). A thorough study evidenced that the fluorescence quenching by ONP and PNP originates from inner filter effect and dynamic quenching. Additionally, the quenching sphere of action contributes to the quenching mechanisms at high concentrations of ONP. The fluorescence nanoprobe shows excellent linearity over wide dynamic ranges of 0.30–60.0 and 0.20–40.0 µg/mL with limits of quantification of 0.23 and 0.11 µg/mL and limits of detection of 0.07 and 0.03 µg/mL for ONP and PNP, respectively. The sensing system was fully validated and successfully used for detection of ONP and PNP in real samples from tap, river, and rainwaters showing excellent selectivity and recovery (98.5–102.0%). A smartphone image processing-assisted paper-based analytical device has been also designed employing a free software as an easily handled and portable device for fast in-field analysis of environmental waters. Moreover, ImageJ software coupled to a computer was applied for image processing to validate the newly adopted smartphone powered software. Statistical comparison of the data obtained by the two software programs showed no significant difference. This platform is very important and interesting specially for the developing countries lacking the facilities and infrastructure including advanced analytical laboratories and instruments. Such tactic allows these countries to keep pace with the increasing global requirements for environmental and human protection.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrophenols are artificial chemicals that do not occur naturally in the environment. Among this group of compounds, o-nitrophenol (ONP) and p-nitrophenol (PNP) have been itemized as priority water pollutants by the USA Environmental Protection Agency [1] since both are toxic chemicals that are globally produced in significant quantities and frequently occurring in water; thus, both are considered as dangerous and hazardous contaminants in the aquatic system. ONP and PNP are released to the environment from industrial wastewater discharges where they are commonly used as intermediates in the synthesis of pharmaceutical compounds like paracetamol and phenacetin, insecticides like parathion and phosalon, and azo dyes. They are also used in leather, rubber, and paper industries. In addition, both are released to the environment from gasoline and diesel vehicles, and decomposition of the insecticides and pesticides. The deposition of airborne nitrophenols with rainwaters has been reported in many researches and known to contribute to their levels in the soil. In addition, people may be exposed to these compounds by the consumption of agricultural products treated with respective precursor pesticides. Meanwhile, industrial workers are very likely to experience higher doses of nitrophenols [2]. Human exposure to these nitrophenols leads to many complications such as endocrine disruption, antiandrogen effects [3,4], methemoglobinemia, and respiratory disorders. In addition, nitrophenols induce toxic effects in plants, aquatic organisms, and animals [2]. Thus, monitoring of these compounds in the environment hold a great attention with a special interest in developing fast and reliable methods.

The reported methods for determination of ONP and PNP include GC–MS [5,6], HPLC [7,8], capillary electrophoresis [9], spectrophotometry [10], spectrofluorimetry [11,12], and chemiluminescence [13]. Nevertheless, these methods ordinarily necessitate either multi-step derivatization/synthesis [11–13], complicated instruments [5,6], or lengthy analysis time [5–13]. In addition, some of these methods lack the satisfactory sensitivity [8,10]. For these reasons, some researchers have synthesized fluorescence probes based on nanomaterial for the detection of nitrophenols. For examples, bovine serum albumin capped Ag nanoclusters [14], gold nanoclusters [15], cyclodextrin capped CdTe quantum dots [16], core–shell CdSe@SiO2/carbon dots [17], carbon dots [18–20], silica-coated molecularly imprinted polymer-anchored graphene quantum dots [21], and aminopyrene-functionalized mesoporous silica [22] have been synthesized for detection of PNP. On the other hand, similar researches on ONP are limited. For example, as far as we know, only two nanomaterial-based fluorescence sensors were reported for ONP utilizing cyclodextrin capped CdTe quantum dots [16] and carbon dots [20]. However, the application of such methods for detection of ONP or PNP requires synthesis, purification, and characterization of the specified nanomaterial that might need around 24 h or more. This in turn encumbers their applicability for rapid screening of industrial discharges and target areas. In addition, many of such techniques depend on using metal-based nanomaterials [14–17] which have a great potential cytotoxicity [23]. Thus, in this study we developed a convenient, rapid, and effective method for detection of ONP and PNP using non-toxic, safe, [24], commercially available blue luminescent pristine graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Compared to metal-based nanoprobes and organic sensors, GQDs are characterized by biocompatibility and low toxicity, thus they have been widely used as a sensing probe and also for bioimaging [25]. The strong fluorescence of GQDs at 440 nm (λex 350 nm) was effectively quenched in the presence of ONP or PNP and wide dynamic ranges of 200-fold could be achieved. Moreover, a profound study was conducted to understand the mechanism of fluorescence quenching. The suggested probe exhibited excellent selectivity for the two nitrophenols in environmental water samples from different sources.

In the recent years, many analytical applications have been established depending on smartphone due to its high-quality camera, computational abilities, processor, and easier portability and accessibility than laptop computers. These applications include detection of biological substances, contaminants, and drugs in variable matrices. This is inspiring since smartphone became a vital device in our lives and its users increase from day to day. However, most of these applications depend on self-written programs for analysis of the digital images captured by the smartphone camera [26] which may limit their applicability. This motivated the authors to extend the proposed method for the design of a portable platform adopting a paper-based analytical device impregnated with GQDs and coupled with smartphone image processing via a free software (RGB color detector program) for rapid on-site determination of ONP and PNP in environmental water. The nitrophenol causes quenching of the fluorescence of GQDs-impregnated paper leading to a gradual color variation detected by UV-illumination at 365 nm and recorded by the smartphone camera then converted to a numerical value of signal intensity via image processing software [27]. We also analyzed the images using the frequently used free software; ImageJ coupled with a computer to validate the RGB color detector software and ensure its reliability. It is worthy to mention herein that, the ImageJ software is not working on smartphones.

GQD suspension (1 mg/mL in water, Lot # MKCK8189), with a certified quantum yield > 65% and diameter < 5 nm, was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, USA). PNP (>99%), ONP (>98%), and 4-chloro-o-cresol were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industries Co. (Tokyo). o-Chlorobenzoic acid and m-bromobenzoic acid were products of Wako Pure Chemical Industries, Ltd. (Osaka) and 1-chloro-2,4-dinitrobenzene was produced by Nacalai Tesque (Kyoto, Japan). Milli-Q® integral water purification system from Merck Millipore (Darmstadt, Germany) was used to obtain Milli-Q water to be used throughout the study. Advantec filter papers (150 mm) from Toyo Roshi Kaisha, Ltd. (Tokyo) were used.

GQD (10.0 µg/mL), PNP, and ONP (1.44 mM) solutions were prepared in deionized water. 0.01 M solutions of 1-chloro-2,4-dinitrobenzene, 4-chloro-o-cresol, o-chlorobenzoic acid, and m-bromobenzoic acid were prepared in methanol. Solutions of KI, KF, Li2SO4·H2O, MgSO4·7H2O, CaCl2·2H2O, NaCl, KCl, Na2CO3, glycine, and glucose (0.01 M) were prepared in deionized water. Borate buffer (0.04 M) was prepared by mixing suitable volumes of 0.04 M boric acid and 0.04 M NaOH to obtain pH 9.5.

Accurate volumes of GQDs (500 µL, 10 µg/mL), 0.04 M borate buffer (500 µL), and increasing volumes of ONP (15 µL-3 mL) or PNP (10 µL-2 mL) standard solutions (1.44 mM) were mixed in a group of 10 mL volumetric flasks. The solutions were diluted with deionized water to make up the final volumes to achieve final concentrations of 2.16–431.4 µM (0.3–60.0 µg/mL) of ONP and 1.44–287.6 µM (0.2–40.0 µg/mL) of PNP. After mixing the solutions, measurement of the fluorescence intensity (F) was done at 440 nm after excitation at 350 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the blank (F0) was also measured by eliminating the nitrophenols from the experiment. Each concentration was determined in triplicate and the average values of F were utilized in further calculations. The calibration graphs were built by plotting Ln F0/F against concentration [M] and the regression equations were derived.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for analysis of real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water was obtained from our laboratory, river water was from Urakami river, Nagasaki City, Japan, and rainwater sample was collected from Nagasaki City (22 December 2019). All water samples were filtered through 0.2 µm cellulose acetate syringe-driven filter (LMS Co. Ltd., Tokyo) and kept at 4 °C till analysis. 2.0 mL of each water sample was transferred into 10 mL volumetric flask, mixed with 500 µL of GQDs (10 µg/mL) and 500 µL of 0.04 M borate buffer, made up to volume by deionized water, mixed well, and the fluorescence intensity was measured at λex/λem of 350/440 nm. For spiking experiments, 2.0 mL of water samples were spiked with different concentrations of ONP (0.5,1.0, and 2.0 µg/mL) and PNP (0.2, 0.8, and 1.0 µg/mL) before proceeding as previously described. Three independent determinations were conducted for each concentration and the average values of F were utilized to calculate the %recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of paper-based sensor coupled with image processing as a fast analysis tool for ONP and PNP
ce:section.ce:para: A cellulose filter paper was cut into rectangles (1 × 4 cm), dipped into GQDs solution (10.0 µg/mL) for 15 min, stirred from time to time to avoid settling, then removed and dried in a drying oven at 50 °C to obtain the paper-based probes. The paper-based probes were spiked with 200 µL of pH 9.5-buffered nitrophenol solutions of variable concentrations (10.0, 20.0, 40.0, 60.0 µg/mL of ONP and 4.0, 8.0, 20.0, and 40.0 µg/mL of PNP) using a micropipette, then allowed to dry at room temperature. Visual detection was done by illuminating the paper-based probes under UV-lamp at 365 nm in a closed cabinet. The images were captured using Samsung Galaxy S9 smartphone camera keeping a constant distance between the camera and the paper in all experiments. The images (JPEG format) were processed using the smartphone powered RGB color detector free software or transferred to a computer and processed by ImageJ free software to obtain the signal intensity (S). A 1.0-cm2 sample size area was used for determination of the signal intensity for each experiment. Triplicate determinations were adopted per each concentration and a blank experiment was done simultaneously omitting analytes and its signal intensity (S0) was calculated by the software. The calibration graphs were prepared by drawing S0/S versus analyte concentration and the regression equations were derived.

FTIR spectroscopy was utilized to recognize the surface functional groups of the GQDs. As seen in Fig. 1 A, the broad peak at 3365 cm−1 is corresponding to stretching vibration of –OH (alcoholic or phenolic), while the peaks at 1054 and 1394 cm−1 are corresponding to C-OH stretching of primary alcohol and O–H bending of alcohol, respectively. This indicates the presence of surface –OH groups that conveys good water solubility and stability. The peak at 1643 cm−1 is corresponding to C = C stretching of conjugated alkene of the sp2 hybridized graphitic cores, while the peaks at 2900 and 2980 cm−1 are attributed to C–H stretching of alkane. The peak at 1230 cm−1 is attributed to C-O stretching of alkyl aryl ether [28]. The result of zeta potential measurement agreed well with these results, where it shows a negative value at −26 mV (Fig. 1B) that is attributed to the negatively charged hydroxyl groups on the surface of GQDs. The surface negative charges cause high stability and prevent aggregation of the GQDs as a result of the electrostatic repulsion.

The optical characters of the GQDS were elucidated by UV–VIS and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV spectrum (Fig. 1C) of GQDs shows two peaks at 240 and 350 nm assigning π-π* transition of C = C conjugated system (core band) and n-π* transition of C-O (edge band), respectively. The GQDs has an intense blue fluorescence with maximum excitation/emission wavelengths at 350/440 nm (Fig. 1C). Changing the λex over the range of 300–400 nm, the fluorescence maxima appeared at 440 without spectral shift (Fig. S1A, Supplementary material). This excitation-independent performance is attributed to the uniform particle size and less exterior defeats [29].

The dependence of GQDs fluorescence on the pH was also studied. As presented in Fig. S1B (Supplementary material), the fluorescence intensity of GQDs shows great inhibition at pH < 4.0 and maximum signal over the pH range of 5.3–10.5. At pH values lower than 4.0, the surface –OH groups are nonionized and the net surface charge decreases causing particles aggregation and fluorescence inhibition. While at pH > 4.0, the surface –OH groups are deprotonated and associated with a high net surface negative charge; thus, the suspension remains stable with high fluorescence intensity.

The GQDs display intense fluorescence that is remarkably quenched in the presence of ONP or PNP as illustrated in Fig. 2 A & 2B. The fluorescence quenching may originate from a diversity of mechanisms such as inner filter effect (IFE), florescence energy transfer (FRET), dynamic quenching, or static quenching [30]. To elucidate the feasible mechanism(s) that contribute to the quenching of GQDs fluorescence by the two studied compounds, we followed some systematic steps as illustrated in Scheme 1 A.

First, the UV-absorption spectra of the quenchers and the excitation and emission spectra of GQDs were scanned. As obvious in Fig. 2C, the absorption spectra of ONP and PNP are strongly overlapped with both the excitation and emission spectra of GQDs, thus IFE (primary and secondary) or FRET might occur [30]. In order to further elucidate this theory, each fluorescence intensity value of GQD in presence of increasing concentrations of the quenchers, ONP and PNP, was corrected for possible IFE according to Eq. (1) [31]:
(1)
F
corr
=
F
obs
× a n t i l o g [
A
ex
+
A
em
2
]

Where Fobs is the observed fluorescence intensity, Fcorr is the corrected fluorescence intensity after omitting IFE from Fobs, Aex and Aem are the absorbance values of the quencher at the excitation and emission wavelengths of the fluorophore, respectively.

Then the suppressed efficiency (%E) was calculated for both corrected and observed fluorescence according to Eq. (2):
(2)
% E = [ 1 -
(
F
F 0
)
] × 100

Plots of %E of the observed and corrected fluorescence of GQD-quencher versus quencher concentration [M] (Fig. 2D & 2E) inferring that the IFE is a major player in the fluorescence quenching by about 60% and 45% of the total suppressed efficiency for PNP and ONP, respectively. Another evident that supports this result is the gradual red shift of the fluorescence maxima at high quencher concentrations (Fig. 2 A & 2B) that is a known phenomenon attributed to the larger IFE [32]. The higher contribution of IFE in the total suppressed efficiency in case of PNP than in case of ONP is attributed to its higher UV-absorption (Fig. 2C).

In the meantime, there is other mechanism(s) that might be responsible for the remaining portion of quenching. In the next steps for elucidation of these mechanisms, we used the corrected fluorescence in all calculations to exclude the IFE. To further explore the allied quenching mechanisms, Stern-Volmer study was conducted at 293, 303, and 313 K according to the Stern-Volmer equation [30]:
(3)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
D
[ Q ]

Where KD is the Stern-Volmer dynamic quenching constant and [Q] is the molar concentration of quencher. The obtained results displayed that the Stern-Volmer graph of PNP is linear over the concentration range of 0–215.7 µM (Fig. 2F) at the three studied temperature settings indicating a single mechanism of fluorescence quenching. In addition, we found that the slope of Stern-Volmer graph (KD) is dependent on the temperature where the KD value increases regularly with increasing of the temperature (Table 1 ). This behavior is a characteristic of dynamic quenching since higher temperatures leads to faster diffusion and greater collisional quenching [30].

On the other hand, the behavior of ONP was different where the Stern-Volmer graph is concaved showing an ascendant curvature towards the y-axis at high concentration for all the studied temperatures (Fig. 2G). This behavior indicates that, the GQDs are affected by two quenching mechanisms at high concentration of ONP. However, the quenching data are fitted to Stern-Volmer equation over the concentration range of 0–100.66 µM (Fig. 2G inset) with good linearity (r2 = 0.9675–0.9972) and the KD values showed a stepwise increase with the increase in the temperature (Table 1) indicating dynamic quenching. Thus, one of the two involved quenching mechanisms is dynamic quenching while the other might be static quenching via formation of a dark ground-state complex or quenching sphere of action that reduces the fraction of fluorescent molecules [30].

As a way for defining whether static quenching participates in the quenching mechanisms or not, the following modified Stern-Volmer equation was applied to examine the obtained data [30]:
(4)
[
F 0
F
- 1 ]
1 / Q =
(
K D
+
K S
)
+
K D
.
K S
Q

Where KS is the static quenching constant. This equation is 2nd order in [Q] which is responsible for the great increase in the quenching at high [Q] when both dynamic and static quenching are involved. Therefore, if such relationship is valid to describe our case, a graph of
[
F 0
F
- 1 ] 1 / Q
versus [Q] should show a linear correlation with a straight line and a slope = KD.KS and intercept = KD + KS [25,28]. Though, it was recognized that the experimental data are not fitted to this equation and the correlation coefficients were very poor. This means that static quenching cannot be a part of the quenching mechanisms [33]. Another evidence that confirms the exclusion of static quenching originates from comparing the UV-absorption spectrum of GQDs recorded using water as a reference and the UV-absorption spectrum of GQDs-ONP using ONP as a reference. As clearly seen in Fig. 2H, the two spectra are perfectly coincided, evidencing that no ground-state complexation toke place [30]. The formation of a ground state complex is hindered by the repulsion between the negatively charged GQDs (zeta potential equals −26 mV) and the phenoxide anion of ONP, exists in the alkaline pH, that made it impossible for ONP and GQDs to bind via electrostatic interaction. The repulsive force excludes also the opportunity of FRET mechanism.

Hence, the data was evaluated using the model of quenching sphere of action. The quenching sphere of action is a form of apparent static quenching happens when the quencher gets close to the fluorophore, within the “sphere of action”, at the excitation moment. The adjacent fluorophore-quencher duo seems as imaginary non-fluorescent pair (Scheme 1B). Such case is expressed by the next modified form of Stern-Volmer equation [30,33]:
(5)
F
0
F
=
( 1 +
K
D
[ Q ]
)
e x p
(
[ Q ]
V )

Where V is the volume of the sphere of action (M−1), N is Avogadro’s number = 6.02 × 1023. By fitting the data shown in Fig. 2G to Eq. (5), the V values were calculated at different temperature settings (Table 1). The radius of the sphere of action (R) was then calculated using the following equation [34]:
(6)
V =
N 1000
×
4 3
× π
R
3

Where: the factor of 1000 is essential for correcting units when the molar concentration is used. The term N/1000 converts molarity to molecules/cm3 [30]. The obtained data are collected in Table 1. The sphere radii were in the range of 77.6 to 84.3 A° which is larger than the summation of the radii of GQDs and ONP, where the manufacturer certified that the diameter of GQDs is < 5 nm representing a radius of < 2.5 nm (<25 A°); meanwhile, the radius of ONP was calculated based on the mass and density concept [35] and it equaled to 3.33 A° and by Chem3D Ultra software and it equaled to 2.83 A° in the dynamic state and 2.65 A° in the static state. This means that when the GQDs and ONP became that close, there is a high chance that its fluorescence is quenched before diffusing apart (Scheme 1B) [30]. It is worthy remarking that, elevating the temperature causes an increase in the KD, V, and R (Table 1). The elevated temperature facilitates the diffusion of the molecules and persuades greater collision, thus increases the KD and leads to upsurge in the volume of the sphere of action within which there is a likelihood of quenching [33].

Assembling all the evidences discussed above, it becomes clear that the quenching of GQDs fluorescence by PNP is a result of IFE and dynamic quenching while the quenching by ONP is mediated by a combination of IFE, dynamic quenching, and quenching sphere of action that came to effect at high concentration. Scheme 1B illustrates the three quenching mechanisms. Although ONP and PNP are isomers with similar properties, the quenching sphere of action is observed in case of ONP not in case of PNP since the diffusion coefficient of ONP is greater than PNP (0.536 × 10-9 m2.s−1 for ONP and 0.474 × 10-9 m2.s−1 for PNP) and the molecular size of ONP < PNP [36]. This means that ONP can rapidly diffuse and approach GQDs at high concentration causing quenching by the sphere of action.

To accomplish a sensitive detection of PNP and ONP, the experimental conditions were carefully studied. In this investigation, the fluorescence quenching efficiency is defined as F0/F. The influence of pH on the sensing system was examined over the range of 3.8–12.3. The experiments showed that, the fluorescence quenching increases steadily with the increase in pH up to pH 8.8 then a plateau is attained (Fig. 3 A). Since ONP and PNP are known by shifting of their UV-absorption spectra with pH changing from acidic to alkaline, the increased fluorescence quenching efficiency is attributed to the increase of the absorbance at λem (ONP) and λem and λex (PNP) (Fig. S1, Supplementary material) thus enhancing the IFE. Borate buffer of pH 9.5 was eventually selected as the optimal in this study. The change in the volume of buffer has no significant effect on F0/F, 500 µL was used in further studies. The concentration of GQDs was also investigated (0.2–0.6 µg/mL), and a concentration of 0.5 µg/mL was chosen as the optimum (Fig. 3B). Different solvents such as water, methanol, and ethanol were also investigated, and it was found that water is the best solvent to perform the study in terms of simplicity, safety, cost-efficiency, and availability while the three solvents gave quite similar fluorescence quenching efficiency.

The developed method was validated by following the instruction of ICH Q2(R1) for validation of analytical methods [37]. Linearity, range, limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ), accuracy, precision, robustness, and selectivity were carefully examined and reported herein.

The fluorescence intensity of GQDs decreased gradually in the presence of increasing concentrations of ONP and PNP. Plots of F0/F versus concentration of the nitrophenols [M] showed exponential growth over the concentration ranges of 2.16–431.4 and 1.44–287.6 µM, while a linear relationship exits over the ranges of 2.16–100.66 and 1.44–71.9 µM with r2 of 0.9963 and 0.9971 (Fig. 4 A & B) for ONP and PNP, respectively. Meanwhile, plots of Ln F0/F versus concentration [M] exhibited excellent linearity over the entire ranges of 2.16–431.4 and 1.44–287.6 µM (Fig. 4C & 4D). This kind of fluorescence-quencher’s concentration relationship is a typical characteristic associated with significant IFE mechanism [31].

The LODs were calculated based on 3σ/a, and LOQs were calculated as 10σ/a where σ is the standard deviation of the blank (n = 6) and a is the slope of the calibration curve. The obtained results including the regression equations, correlation coefficients, ranges, LODs, and LOQs, are collected in Table 2 .

The accuracy and precision (intra- and inter-day) of the proposed method were assessed by analyzing three concentrations of both analytes (2.88, 21.57, and 100.66 µM) three times in the same day and on three consecutive days. The inter-day recoveries were in the ranges of 98.26–99.40% and 98.80–100.35% and %RSD does not exceed 1.01 and 1.21%, while the intra-day recoveries laid in the ranges of 101.30–101.99 and 98.26–101.01% with %RSD ≤ 1.40 and 1.45% for ONP and PNP, respectively. These results confirmed the accuracy and precision of the proposed nanoprobe. A summary of these results is collected in Table 2. Furthermore, the suggested method exhibited high robustness since no significant change in the analytical response was detected under slight changes in the experimental conditions such as pH (9.5 ± 0.3), buffer volume (500 ± 100 µL), and GQDs concentration (0.50 ± 0.01 µg/mL).

Selectivity is a crucial factor to assess a fluorescence probe performance, especially when applied for environmental sensing due to the complexity of such matrix and the possibility of existence of many contaminants. Thus, to judge the specificity of the developed nanoprobe, the possible interference from different potentially co-existing substances was investigated. As recognizable from Table 3 , no significant change in F0/F of GQD-nitrophenol system was detectable in the presence of different cations such as Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Na+, K+, and Li+, anions such as SO4 2-, I-, Cl-, F-, and CO3 2–, organic molecules like glucose and glycine, aromatic species like o-chlorobenzoic acid, and m-bromobenzoic acid, phenolic species such as 4-chloro-o-cresol, or nitroaromatic compound such as 1-chloro-2,4-dinitrobenzene. The obtained results suggested that the %quenching efficiency change caused by co-existing substances did not exceed 5.6%. Therefore, the new probe possesses adequate selectivity for ONP and PNP that enables its application in environmental analysis. This selectivity is attributed to the dependence of the sensing system on a unique combination of mechanisms, including IFE, dynamic quenching, and quenching sphere of action, that is difficult to be exhibited collectively by other molecules. Despite some of the tested cations, such as Cu2+ and Fe3+, have been previously determined using some kinds of doped GQDs, these cations showed no interference under the optimum detection conditions. This is attributed to the likely precipitation of Cu2+ at pH 6.5–12 [38] and Fe3+ at pHs > 4.5 [39], thus they are excluded from the reaction medium of optimum pH 9.5. In addition, the detection of Cu2+, Hg2+, and Fe3+ necessitates nitrogen-doped or sulfur-doped GQDs to interact with the cation [40–44].

The results of validation study established the feasibility of the proposed probe for determination of ONP and PNP in the environmental waters. Furthermore, the probe in this work is easily accessible excluding the need for synthesis and purification steps. Besides, the procedure is very simple, and the analytical response is instantaneous. Table 4 illustrates a comparison of the developed probe with some published relevant fluorescence-based methods showing special advantages of time effectiveness and simplicity, in addition to better or comparable sensitivity. The use of pristine GQDs free from doping or metal nanoparticles incorporation is another advantage that adds to the method’s safety and greenness in contrary to most of the published literature employing metal nanoparticles [14–17,22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of the designed probe for determination of ONP and PNP in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: According to the recommendations of the U.S Environmental Protection Agency, the concentrations of the ONP and PNP in fresh and saltwater should not exceed 0.55 and 0.12 µg/mL, respectively, at any moment for the safety of aquatic lives [45]. Despite the U.S Environmental Protection Agency lacks a specific criterion for protection of human health from the intake of ONP and PNP in polluted water or food, we can refer to the limit settled for dinitrophenols (0.07 µg/mL) [46]. Furthermore, high levels of nitrophenols were detected in rainwater in some countries (up to 0.1984 µg/mL). Since all these limits are within the detectability of the developed probe (LOD = 0.07 and 0.03 µg/mL for ONP and PNP, respectively), we further tested its practical applicability for the quantification of ONP and PNP in different real water samples such as tap, river, and rainwaters. The results showed the absence of both compounds in the examined water samples. In turn, the spiking technique has been conducted for determination of the two nitrophenols in the three water samples by adding different concentrations to test the recovery and examine the matrix effect. As Table 5 revealed, the detected concentrations were very close to the spiked ones with %recoveries ranged from 98.60 to 101.50 and 98.5–102.0% for ONP and PNP, respectively.

In order to compare the performance of the new probe in different matrices (deionized water, river water, tap water, and rainwater) in terms of accuracy and precision, Brown-Forsythe test was used to examine for the equality of variances and it confirmed that all the tested matrices showed equal variances indicating no difference in the method precision in different matrices (Table 6 ). Thus, the ANOVA test was used to compare the mean of the different groups. The four groups have no significant difference between their means which was further confirmed using the post hoc Dunnett's multiple comparisons test for comparing each tested matrix with the control matrix; deionized water (Table 6). All the statistical tests were two-sided and considered non-significant at probability p > 0.05 [47].

These results obviously depict that, the new probe is promising for determination of the two nitrophenols in various water samples of different natures. Meanwhile, differentiation of ONP and PNP in real water samples can be accomplished depending on the magnitude of dynamic quenching constant (KD) [48]. It is obvious that, the values of KD obtained for ONP are significantly smaller than the corresponding values for PNP (threefold smaller) (Table 1). Thus, in case of detecting a candidate nitrophenol in a polluted water sample by the developed method, a Stern-Volmer plot can be constructed using increasing volumes of the polluted water (e.g. 2.0, 4.0, and 8.0 mL) and correcting the fluorescence values for IFE. Then, by comparing the KD value obtained from the slope of this Stern-Volmer plot with the values obtained for ONP and PNP standards, the actual candidate exists in the water sample can be determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fast analysis of ONP and PNP using paper-based analytical device assisted with image processing via smartphone- or computer-powered free software
ce:section.ce:para: For the best benefiting from the simplicity, rapidness, and selectivity of the new sensing system, we fabricated a paper-based sensor for detection of both ONP and PNP in water samples (Scheme 2 ). In the absence of nitrophenols, the GQDs-paper sensor exhibits a bright blue fluorescence when excited by the UV-lamp at 365 nm. In contrary, when the GQDs-paper spiked with water containing ONP or PNP, the blue fluorescence gradually decreases with the increasing concentration of the nitrophenol. This clearly demonstrates the ability of the paper-based sensor for rapid and selective in-field detection of ONP and PNP.

Since it is our main goal to make this paper-based analytical device approach as fast, accessible, and portable as possible, we were motivated to proceed with the image processing technique to analyze the digital images captured by the smartphone built-in camera under illumination with UV-lamp at 365 nm. However, we found that, the applications of smartphone in scientific researches as a detector depends mainly on self-designed programs [26]. This, in turn, encumbers the wide applicability of this technique and limits its use to whom have such programs. Thus, we decided to adopt the RGB color detector Apps recently released in 2018 and freely available in google play store for image processing for the first time. Furthermore, for confirming the validity, reliability, and accuracy of the results of such program, we alternatively processed the digital images using the ImageJ software coupled with a computer then we conducted a statistical comparison between the obtained data to inspect any significant difference between the two programs. ImageJ software is one of the most frequently utilized software by researchers for image processing and analysis. However, running ImageJ on smartphones is not currently working [49].

We found that, image processing via either ImageJ software using a computer or RGB color detector software using a smartphone gave signal intensities (S) that are inversely proportional to the analyte concentrations. The increase in analyte concentration leads to an increase in the fluorescence quenching and corresponding decrease in the color intensity of the paper that is recorded by the smartphone camera and analyzed by the software to be immediately translated to signal intensities (S). In other words; the increase in nitrophenol concentration leads to decrease of S and increase of S0/S, where S0 is the signal intensity of the blank. Excellent linear relationship between the analyte concentration and S0/S with high correlation coefficients were acquired utilizing the two software. Table 7 shows the data for linearity, range, LOD, and LOQ. Good repeatability was also achieved by triplicate analysis of three concentrations of each analyte, where the %RSD values were ≤ 1.89 for all measurements. Additionally, the accuracy of the paper-based analytical device was also investigated by calculating the %recovery for three different concentrations of each analyte. Good %recoveries were obtained for the two analytes using the two image processing programs which highlights the accuracy of this approach for determination of ONP and PNP. The results of this validation study [37] are collected in Table 7.

Hence, the performance of the two adopted image processing programs was statistically compared by Student’s t-test and Variance ratio F-test [50]. We found that, the calculated values of t and F are less than the critical values (Table 7) which means that, no significant difference is encountered between the two programs. Thus, we can conclude that coupling of the smartphone with RGB color detector free software in the developed method is very interesting and pioneering by virtue of excluding the need to a self-designed program. This platform demonstrated very promising results that did not show any significant difference from the results obtained by the ImageJ software; thus, it offers a reliable way to design new sensors for various purposes.

This approach of coupling an easily handled and portable paper-based analytical device with a smartphone-powered free software, or even with a PC-powered software, is very important and interesting specially for the developing countries lacking the facilities and infrastructure including advanced analytical laboratories and instruments. Such tactic allows these countries to keep pace with the increasing global requirements for environmental and human protection.

In this study, we have effectively demonstrated the applicability of pristine GQDs as a selective and fast nanoprobe for detection of ONP and PNP in waters from different sources. ONP quenched the intense fluorescence of GQDs by a combination of IFE, dynamic quenching, and a quenching sphere of action. On the other hand, PNP quenched the fluorescence via IFE and dynamic quenching. Wide dynamic ranges of 200-fold were achieved with LODs of 0.50 and 0.21 µM (0.07 and 0.03 µg/mL) for ONP and PNP, respectively. Furthermore, the developed nanoprobe was effectively utilized for determination of ONP and PNP in tap water, river water, and rainwater with excellent recoveries. In addition, a paper-based analytical device has been fabricated and coupled with the free image processing software powered by a smartphone, RGB color detector software, for the first time for determination of nitrophenols. The newly adopted RGB color detector software is perfectly comparable with the commonly used ImageJ software/computer system. Such approach is promising and valuable specially for developing countries where insufficiency of laboratories and assistant tools is usually an obstacle. The availability of the used GQDs and software in addition to the selectivity, rapidness, and simplicity of the analytical procedure pave an innovative way for detection of these two endocrine disrupting chemicals in different aquatic systems of variable matrices.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Rania El-Shaheny: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Sakura Yoshida: Investigation, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Takeshi Fuchigami: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105241.

ce:section.ce:para:
